patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 68-year-old retired shipyard pipefitter. You speak clearly but are anxious and worried about costs and what goes on your record. Start with: “I’ve been getting more and more short of breath, especially when I walk or climb stairs.” Volunteer early: you get winded on one flight of stairs, have a persistent dry cough, and feel more tired lately. If asked, say it has slowly worsened over years; you sometimes get a tight/heavy chest feeling with exertion that goes away with rest; mild unintentional weight loss and low appetite; no fever/chills, no blood in sputum, little wheeze; cold air triggers breathlessness. Only reveal if specifically asked about work/exposures or sleeping: years around dusty old insulation/pipe wrap with poor masks; worse when lying flat so you sleep propped up; occasional ankle puffiness; fingertips look more rounded. Be reluctant/embarrassed about smoking a few cigarettes daily; admit only if asked directly. Mention your own theories: aging/out of shape, asthma, reflux, or COPD. If clinician is empathetic, you soften, share fears about insurance, expensive tests/oxygen, and avoiding workers’ comp complications.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Asbestosis (asbestos-related interstitial lung disease with pulmonary fibrosis)
required_evidence: History of significant asbestos exposure with appropriate latency (typically 10–20+ years after exposure)
Progressive exertional dyspnea +/- dry cough
Bibasilar end-inspiratory crackles on exam (often with reduced chest expansion)
Restrictive pattern on pulmonary function tests with reduced DLCO
Imaging consistent with interstitial fibrosis (HRCT: subpleural/basal reticulation, traction bronchiectasis; may have honeycombing in advanced disease)
Supportive asbestos markers: pleural plaques and/or pleural thickening on imaging
Exclusion of alternative causes of ILD (e.g., idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis, hypersensitivity pneumonitis, connective tissue disease-associated ILD)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Usually normal; may be normal to mildly low if chronic disease/comorbidity (no specific diagnostic pattern for asbestosis)
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Typically normal (no leukocytosis unless concurrent infection/inflammation)
expected_lab_results.CMP - Bicarbonate (CO2): Often normal; may be mildly elevated if chronic CO2 retention from advanced lung disease (not typical early)
expected_lab_results.CMP - Creatinine: Typically normal (baseline needed before certain therapies and for general safety)
expected_lab_results.CMP - AST: Typically normal (baseline if considering medications with hepatic risk)

medications: Influenza vaccine 0.5 mL IM once annually
Pneumococcal vaccine PCV20 0.5 mL IM once (or PCV15 0.5 mL IM once then PPSV23 0.5 mL IM once ≥1 year later, per age/risk schedule)
Oxygen therapy (supplemental O2) titrate to maintain SpO2 ≥ 88-92% (route: nasal cannula) continuous or exertional as indicated by resting/exertional hypoxemia
non_pharmacological: Immediate and permanent avoidance of further asbestos exposure; occupational medicine referral if ongoing exposure risk
Smoking cessation counseling and pharmacotherapy options as appropriate (if smoker)
Pulmonary rehabilitation for dyspnea and functional limitation
Assess need for ambulatory oxygen with exertion and nocturnal oximetry if symptoms suggest
Baseline and periodic pulmonary function tests (spirometry, lung volumes, DLCO)
High-resolution CT chest if not already performed to characterize fibrosis and pleural disease
Screen for and manage complications: pulmonary hypertension, chronic respiratory failure
Cancer risk counseling: increased risk of lung cancer and mesothelioma; ensure age- and risk-appropriate lung cancer screening (low-dose CT) when criteria met
Consider referral to pulmonology; consider lung transplant evaluation in advanced disease with progressive decline despite optimal supportive care
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Pulmonology follow-up in 4-8 weeks (earlier in 1-2 weeks if hypoxemic, rapidly progressive symptoms, or new/worsening findings), then every 3-6 months with PFT trend and symptom monitoring

must_include: Asbestosis is scarring (fibrosis) of the lungs caused by past asbestos exposure and usually develops years after exposure
Condition is typically chronic and may slowly progress; symptoms often include exertional shortness of breath and dry cough
Imaging and breathing tests help confirm the diagnosis and assess severity
Discuss increased risks: lung cancer and mesothelioma, and importance of monitoring
No medication reliably reverses established scarring; treatment focuses on symptom control, preventing complications, and slowing functional decline
Avoid further asbestos exposure and stop smoking to reduce progression and cancer risk
Pulmonary rehab and exercise training can improve function and breathlessness
Oxygen may be needed if oxygen levels are low at rest or with exertion; explain targets and safe use
Vaccinations reduce risk of severe respiratory infections
Clarify prognosis depends on extent of fibrosis, oxygen needs, and lung function trends
Review red flags requiring urgent care: worsening shortness of breath, chest pain, hemoptysis, fever, new leg swelling, or syncope
Discuss workplace/compensation resources if exposure was occupational and offer support/referrals
Smoking cessation (if applicable) and avoidance of secondhand smoke
Infection prevention: hand hygiene, vaccines, early evaluation for respiratory infections
Energy conservation and pacing strategies; maintain activity within tolerance
Home oxygen safety if prescribed (no smoking/open flames; proper storage and use)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for suspected asbestosis: asbestos exposure details (job/tasks, duration, intensity, protective equipment), latency, respiratory symptoms, functional limitation, smoking history, comorbidities, meds, and red flags.

Order appropriate diagnostic evaluation to support/confirm asbestosis and assess severity/complications (e.g., PFTs with DLCO, resting/exertional oximetry or ABG, chest imaging such as CXR/HRCT as appropriate).
Critical safety check before any prescribing (e.g., bronchodilators, antitussives, smoking cessation pharmacotherapy, vaccines/medications if applicable).
Non-critical but expected check prior to prescribing to avoid clinically significant drug-drug interactions (especially if considering smoking cessation meds or symptomatic therapies).

Review ordered test results to confirm restrictive physiology/reduced DLCO, imaging findings consistent with interstitial fibrosis/pleural plaques, and oxygenation status to guide treatment and counseling.

Make/confirm diagnosis of asbestosis based on exposure history, latency, imaging, and pulmonary function findings; consider and document key differentials (IPF, COPD, heart failure) and why less likely.

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment focus, risks (lung cancer/mesothelioma), prognosis factors, red flags, workplace/comp resources, and lifestyle/infection prevention/oxygen safety.

Initiate indicated supportive therapy based on severity (e.g., supplemental oxygen if hypoxemic; smoking cessation pharmacotherapy if applicable; vaccinations if implemented via prescribing workflow), ensuring allergy and interaction checks were completed.
Arrange follow-up for symptom monitoring, oxygen reassessment, PFT trend review, rehab referral, and cancer risk surveillance counseling; include return precautions.
Close encounter after ensuring understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

minimal_paths.description: Standard evaluation confirming asbestosis with exposure history plus imaging/PFTs, then supportive management with education and follow-up.
must_collect: asbestos exposure history (job/tasks, duration, intensity, PPE)
exertional shortness of breath severity and functional limitation
oxygenation symptoms (rest/exertion), prior oximetry if known
red flags (chest pain, hemoptysis, fever, syncope, leg swelling)

steps: ORDER_LAB: PFTs with DLCO; resting and exertional oximetry (±ABG); chest imaging (CXR and/or HRCT per scenario)
EDUCATE: diagnosis + treatment focus + cancer/mesothelioma risk + prognosis factors + red flags + workplace resources + lifestyle/infection prevention/oxygen safety
PRESCRIBE: supportive therapies as indicated (e.g., oxygen if hypoxemic; smoking cessation aids if applicable; vaccines if via prescribing)

Agent prioritizes contraindication assessment explicitly before ordering tests/therapies (e.g., kidney disease/contrast allergy, pregnancy status when relevant, CO2 retention risk when considering oxygen), then orders diagnostics.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 46-year-old female project coordinator. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start with: “I can’t sleep most nights, and it’s starting to mess with my days.” If the clinician asks open-endedly about sleep, volunteer: trouble falling asleep, long time awake after bed, frequent awakenings, unrefreshing sleep, daytime fatigue/brain fog, worse focus at work. If asked details, say it’s been ~2 months, 4–5 nights/week; your mind races about work/to-dos; you’re more irritable; you sometimes nap late afternoon; you drink coffee, sometimes in the afternoon; you scroll on your phone in bed; your sleep/wake times vary. Do not mention unless specifically asked about substances/OTC meds/lifestyle: evening alcohol a few nights/week “to help,” occasional OTC sleep aid with morning grogginess, weight gain, little exercise/sunlight. If asked about mood/anxiety or drugs, be mildly reluctant but admit feeling on edge and using cannabis gummies some nights. Mention heart pounding/sweats after bad dreams and morning headaches if asked about symptoms; assume it’s from poor sleep. Express concern therapy/sleep studies cost too much; believe medication is the only affordable option. If the clinician shows empathy, acknowledge it and stay engaged.

medications: Aprobarbital 200 mg PO at bedtime as needed for sleep
Sertraline 50 mg PO daily
Amlodipine 5 mg PO daily

Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 2000 IU PO daily
confounders: SSRI-associated insomnia (sertraline)
Perimenopausal symptoms with nocturnal hot flashes

Possible subclinical hyperthyroidism (low TSH) contributing to insomnia and palpitations
Alcohol use in evenings to induce sleep (worsens sleep maintenance)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Insomnia disorder (chronic insomnia)
required_evidence: Difficulty initiating sleep and/or maintaining sleep and/or early-morning awakening with inability to return to sleep
Associated daytime impairment (e.g., fatigue, sleepiness, impaired concentration, irritability)
Occurs despite adequate opportunity and circumstances for sleep
Not better explained by another sleep disorder (e.g., obstructive sleep apnea, restless legs syndrome), substance/medication effect, or uncontrolled medical/psychiatric condition
Screen for contributing factors: caffeine/alcohol/nicotine use, shift work, stress/anxiety/depression symptoms, pain, nocturia

expected_lab_results.TSH: Within reference range (no evidence of hyperthyroidism as a driver of insomnia)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Within reference range (no significant anemia contributing to fatigue/sleep disruption)
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Within reference range (no iron deficiency suggestive of restless legs syndrome)

medications: Avoid aprobarbital (barbiturate) due to high risk of dependence, overdose, respiratory depression, and drug interactions; not recommended for insomnia
non_pharmacological: Cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (CBT-I) as first-line (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive restructuring)
Sleep hygiene: consistent wake time, limit naps, bed only for sleep/sex, leave bed if unable to sleep after ~20 minutes, avoid screens/bright light 1-2 hours before bed
Limit caffeine after late morning; avoid nicotine near bedtime; avoid alcohol as a sleep aid
Optimize bedroom environment (dark, quiet, cool) and regular daytime exercise (not close to bedtime)
If symptoms suggest OSA (snoring, witnessed apneas, daytime sleepiness) or RLS (urge to move legs, worse at night), evaluate/referral as indicated
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: 2-4 weeks to assess response to CBT-I and reassess for comorbid contributors; sooner (within 1 week) if severe daytime impairment or safety concerns (e.g., drowsy driving)

must_include: Insomnia is a sleep disorder characterized by trouble falling asleep, staying asleep, or waking too early with daytime impact
Confirm adequate opportunity for sleep and discuss common contributors (stress, substances, schedule, medical/psychiatric issues)
Explain that labs help rule out contributors (thyroid disease, anemia/iron deficiency) but insomnia is primarily diagnosed clinically
CBT-I is first-line and has durable benefits
Aprobarbital/barbiturates are not recommended for insomnia due to dependence/overdose risk and next-day impairment
If any medication is considered later, it should be time-limited, lowest effective dose, and paired with CBT-I; review risks (sedation, falls, driving impairment)
Elicit patient goals and concerns (sleep onset vs awakenings, work/safety issues, prior treatments)
Review allergy history: penicillin causes hives; diphenhydramine causes paradoxical agitation—avoid sedating antihistamines for sleep
Discuss safety: avoid drowsy driving and mixing sedatives with alcohol or other CNS depressants
Set a consistent wake time and restrict time in bed to consolidate sleep
Avoid caffeine late in the day and alcohol at night
Use stimulus control (bed only when sleepy; leave bed if awake) and reduce evening screen exposure

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit insomnia phenotype (sleep onset vs maintenance vs early awakening), duration, frequency, daytime impairment, sleep opportunity, schedule, sleep environment, and red flags (OSA, RLS, mania, substance use, suicidality).
Clarify contributors and comorbidities: stress/anxiety/depression, pain, nocturia, GERD, medications/substances (caffeine, alcohol, nicotine, cannabis), and prior sleep aids/behavioral strategies.
Elicit patient goals/concerns (work/safety, drowsy driving, preference for non-medication), and prior treatments tried and response.
Order targeted labs to rule out contributors when clinically reasonable (e.g., TSH, CBC, ferritin/iron studies if symptoms suggest); reinforce that insomnia is primarily a clinical diagnosis.
Review lab results to exclude major contributors (thyroid disease, anemia/iron deficiency) and incorporate into assessment.
Make a clinical diagnosis of insomnia when criteria are met (sleep difficulty with daytime impact despite adequate opportunity), and document likely contributors.
Explain diagnosis in patient-friendly terms, discuss contributors, and set expectations for treatment (CBT-I first-line; avoid inappropriate sedatives).
Provide CBT-I aligned lifestyle/behavioral guidance: consistent wake time, time-in-bed restriction, stimulus control, reduce evening screens, avoid late caffeine and nighttime alcohol.
Before any prescribing, verify allergies, specifically documenting penicillin (hives) and diphenhydramine paradoxical agitation; avoid sedating antihistamines for sleep.
Before any prescribing, screen for drug-drug and drug-substance interactions, especially CNS depressants (alcohol, opioids, benzodiazepines, gabapentinoids) and other sedating agents.
Before any prescribing, assess contraindications (pregnancy, severe hepatic disease for some agents, history of substance use disorder, untreated OSA, fall risk, older age considerations).

If medication is discussed, emphasize time-limited use, lowest effective dose, pairing with CBT-I, and counsel on sedation, falls, next-day impairment, and avoiding alcohol/CNS depressants; explicitly discourage barbiturates (e.g., aprobarbital).
Arrange follow-up to assess response, adherence to CBT-I strategies, review sleep diary, and reconsider need for short-term pharmacotherapy if symptoms persist.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety plan (no drowsy driving), and follow-up plan.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM), symptomatic (suspected obstructive physiology)
acceptable_alternatives: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy with myocardial injury (demand ischemia)
required_evidence: History/physical consistent with HCM symptoms (exertional dyspnea, chest pain, presyncope/syncope, palpitations) and/or systolic murmur that increases with Valsalva/standing
Elevated NT-proBNP consistent with cardiac strain/heart failure physiology
High-sensitivity troponin I normal or mildly elevated without a dynamic rise/fall suggesting type 1 MI
No marked CK elevation suggesting skeletal muscle injury as primary cause of symptoms
CBC and BMP without alternative explanation for symptoms (e.g., severe anemia, major electrolyte derangement)

expected_lab_results.High-sensitivity troponin I: Normal or mildly elevated; if elevated, lacks significant dynamic change over serial testing in uncomplicated HCM
expected_lab_results.NT-proBNP: Elevated (often moderately to markedly elevated) consistent with myocardial strain/diastolic dysfunction
expected_lab_results.Creatine kinase (CK): Normal or mildly elevated; not in rhabdomyolysis range
expected_lab_results.Complete blood count - Hemoglobin: Typically normal (no severe anemia driving symptoms)
expected_lab_results.Complete blood count - WBC: Typically normal (no leukocytosis suggesting infection as primary driver)
expected_lab_results.Basic metabolic panel - Sodium: Typically normal; may be mildly low if volume overloaded
expected_lab_results.Basic metabolic panel - Potassium: Typically normal; ensure not low/high prior to any QT/arrhythmia-relevant therapy

medications: Metoprolol succinate 25 mg PO daily, titrate every 1-2 weeks to symptom control and resting HR ~50-60 bpm as tolerated
If beta-blocker not tolerated or inadequate: Verapamil ER 120 mg PO daily (avoid if severe hypotension or advanced heart failure) OR Diltiazem ER 120 mg PO daily
If persistent symptoms with obstructive physiology despite above and specialist oversight: Disopyramide 100-150 mg PO every 6-8 hours (or ER formulation per local protocol) with AV nodal blocker
non_pharmacological: Cardiology referral for transthoracic echocardiogram with Doppler to assess LV wall thickness, LVOT gradient (rest and provocation), systolic anterior motion, and diastolic function
Risk stratification for sudden cardiac death (family history, syncope, NSVT, maximal wall thickness, LVOT gradient, apical aneurysm, LGE on CMR if obtained) and consider ICD evaluation if high risk
Activity guidance: avoid dehydration and avoid burst/competitive high-intensity exertion until evaluated; allow moderate-intensity activity per cardiology guidance
Avoid preload/afterload reducing agents that can worsen obstruction unless specifically indicated (e.g., nitrates, high-dose diuretics, pure vasodilators); maintain adequate hydration
Family screening recommendation (first-degree relatives) with ECG/echo per guideline intervals
Consider cardiac MRI if echo is inconclusive or for fibrosis assessment; ambulatory rhythm monitoring if palpitations/syncope
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Cardiology follow-up within 1-2 weeks (earlier/ED evaluation immediately if syncope, ongoing chest pain, hemodynamic instability, or significant troponin rise).

must_include: HCM is thickening of the heart muscle that can impair filling and sometimes obstruct blood flow out of the heart
Symptoms can include shortness of breath, chest discomfort, palpitations, dizziness/fainting

First-line symptom control is usually a beta-blocker; goal is to slow heart rate and improve filling
Some medications can worsen obstruction (nitrates/strong vasodilators/high-dose diuretics) and should be avoided unless advised
Plan for cardiology-directed testing and possible escalation (calcium channel blocker, disopyramide, or procedural options) if symptoms persist
Red flags requiring urgent care: fainting, severe or persistent chest pain, shortness of breath at rest, rapid/irregular heartbeat with lightheadedness
Discuss sudden cardiac death risk assessment and how ICD decisions are made
Reinforce that penicillin allergy is noted and will be avoided if antibiotics are ever needed
Maintain hydration and avoid overheating/dehydration
Avoid stimulant drugs and excessive alcohol; review any OTC decongestants/energy supplements
Encourage family screening for first-degree relatives and genetic counseling/testing discussion as appropriate

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit core anxiety symptoms, duration, functional impairment, triggers, and screen for panic, substance/medication causes, and medical red flags; assess suicidality and safety.
Order labs to evaluate common medical contributors (e.g., thyroid disease) when not already documented; supports differential and required milestone about ruling out thyroid disease based on labs.
Review lab results to confirm no thyroid abnormality and support diagnosis explanation.
Document anxiety disorder consistent with GAD, and note differential considerations and why less likely.
Perform allergy check prior to prescribing; specifically confirm penicillin allergy history and reaction and clarify it does not affect paroxetine use.
Perform drug interaction check prior to prescribing; review current meds/substances for MAOIs/linezolid/methylene blue and other serotonergic agents.
If interaction check reveals gaps, explicitly ask about OTC/herbals (e.g., St John’s wort), migraine meds (triptans), opioids (tramadol), cough meds (dextromethorphan), and alcohol/cannabis use.
Initiate SSRI therapy (paroxetine) only after completing critical safety checks; include dosing plan and tapering/discontinuation cautions.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment expectations/side effects, safety warnings (black box, serotonin syndrome, hyponatremia), pregnancy considerations, and lifestyle/CBT plus crisis resources.
Arrange monitoring plan (early follow-up after initiation/dose changes) and specify return precautions for worsening anxiety, panic, suicidality, or functional decline.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety plan, and follow-up.

nl_assertions: Agent assesses for suicidality/self-harm, severe functional impairment, and need for urgent evaluation before initiating outpatient treatment.

Agent elicits duration and pattern of worry/anxiety, associated symptoms (restlessness, fatigue, concentration issues, irritability, muscle tension, sleep disturbance), and impact on work/school/relationships to support GAD-level diagnosis.

Agent explicitly considers and asks about panic disorder symptoms (episodic surges of fear with palpitations, shortness of breath, fear of dying/losing control) and distinguishes them from chronic generalized worry.

Agent screens for substance/medication contributors (caffeine/energy drinks, stimulants, decongestants, thyroid meds, corticosteroids, alcohol/cannabis, withdrawal states) and documents rationale when less likely.
Agent evaluates medical contributors and documents that thyroid disease is not the cause based on labs (TSH/thyroid studies) and absence of supportive clinical features.
Before prescribing, agent completes all critical safety checks: allergy_check, contraindication_check, and drug_interaction_check; failure to do so is a critical error.
Agent confirms penicillin allergy and clarifies reaction type; explains that penicillin allergy does not affect paroxetine use.

Agent checks contraindications relevant to paroxetine/SSRI use (e.g., concurrent or recent MAOI use; linezolid/methylene blue exposure; history of bipolar/mania screening; pregnancy/trying to conceive considerations) before prescribing.
Agent reviews current medications and supplements for serotonergic interactions and provides clear guidance to avoid MAOIs/linezolid/methylene blue and to use caution with other serotonergic drugs.
Agent explains serotonin syndrome warning signs (agitation, confusion, fever, tremor, diarrhea) and provides explicit instructions on when to seek urgent care.
Agent discusses hyponatremia risk and symptoms (confusion, severe headache, weakness), especially for older adults and diuretic users, and provides return precautions.
Agent explains paroxetine as an SSRI with gradual onset (often 2–4 weeks; full effect 6–8 weeks) and sets expectations for monitoring and adjustment.
Agent counsels on common SSRI side effects (GI upset, headache, sleep changes, sweating, sexual dysfunction) and warns about transient early increased anxiety.
Agent advises not to stop paroxetine abruptly and to taper to avoid discontinuation symptoms.
Agent addresses SSRI black box warning: monitors for worsening mood or suicidal thoughts especially early in treatment or with dose changes; provides crisis resources.
Agent discusses pregnancy considerations if applicable (paroxetine associated with fetal risk; consider alternatives if pregnant/trying) and confirms pregnancy status when clinically relevant.
Agent recommends CBT and lifestyle strategies (limit caffeine/stimulants, exercise, sleep regularity) and frames them as evidence-based adjuncts that can reduce medication needs.
Agent provides a clear follow-up plan (timing, what will be monitored, how to contact clinic) and documents shared decision-making.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 41-year-old male IT support specialist. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by saying your feet and lower legs have been getting weaker and you keep tripping. Volunteer: your feet don’t lift like they used to, you catch your toes (worse on uneven ground/stairs), and your calves look thinner. If asked, add: it’s hard to walk on your heels, you sometimes “slap” your foot down, both sides involved but one started first and is still worse, hands mostly OK but jars/keys slightly harder, no real numbness/tingling, slow progression over years, and a family member had “weak ankles”/needed braces. Do not mention unless specifically asked: using handrails, taking stairs one step at a time, avoiding long walks, uneven shoe wear/supportive shoes, route-planning to avoid curbs, near-falls. If asked about falls, minimize at first due to embarrassment; admit a couple public falls only if gently pressed. Express misconceptions: you wondered about a pinched back nerve and “poor circulation” because feet feel tired/cold. Raise cost/work worries about genetic tests, braces, and time off. If clinician shows empathy, relax, thank them, and share more honestly.

confounders: Statin-associated myopathy risk due to atorvastatin use (can elevate CK and cause muscle symptoms)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Distal myopathy, Tateyama type (tibial muscular dystrophy phenotype)
required_evidence: Progressive distal lower-limb weakness (classically ankle dorsiflexors/anterior tibial muscles) with preserved proximal strength early
Slowly progressive course without prominent sensory symptoms
Myopathic pattern suggested by history/exam (no upper motor neuron signs; no significant sensory loss)
Muscle enzymes normal to mildly elevated rather than markedly high (supports chronic genetic myopathy over inflammatory myositis/rhabdomyolysis)
Exclusion of common reversible mimics with available labs (thyroid disease, B12 deficiency, uncontrolled diabetes)
Family history consistent with inherited myopathy OR prior genetic confirmation if available (TTN-related distal myopathy)

expected_lab_results.Creatine kinase (CK): Normal to mildly elevated (often < 5x upper limit of normal)
expected_lab_results.AST: Normal to mildly elevated (may be mildly high from muscle source)
expected_lab_results.ALT: Normal to mildly elevated (may be mildly high from muscle source)
expected_lab_results.HbA1c: Normal or mildly elevated if comorbid diabetes; not explanatory for primary weakness

medications: Acetaminophen 500 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain (max 3000 mg/day)
non_pharmacological: Referral to neuromuscular specialist for confirmation and longitudinal management (consider genetic testing for TTN and related distal myopathy genes if not already confirmed)
Physical therapy focused on maintaining strength, stretching, and energy-conservation techniques; avoid overexertion that causes prolonged post-exercise weakness
Occupational therapy for adaptive strategies and hand/foot function support as needed
Ankle-foot orthosis (AFO) for foot drop and fall prevention if dorsiflexion weakness present
Fall-risk assessment and home safety modifications
Baseline functional assessment (gait, timed up-and-go/6-minute walk as appropriate) and periodic reassessment
Genetic counseling for patient and family (inheritance, testing options, reproductive counseling)
Vaccinations and general health maintenance; encourage low-impact aerobic activity within tolerance
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neuromuscular follow-up in 4-8 weeks after initial evaluation/therapy initiation, then every 6-12 months or sooner if rapid functional decline, new bulbar/respiratory symptoms, or frequent falls

must_include: This condition is a genetic muscle disease causing slowly progressive weakness mainly in distal muscles (often feet/ankles) rather than a nerve problem
Course is typically gradual; severity and progression vary by person
Labs may be normal or only mildly elevated, which fits a chronic inherited myopathy
Discuss need for specialist confirmation and genetic testing/counseling
No curative medication currently; management focuses on function, safety, and symptom control
Role of PT/OT and braces (AFO) to improve gait and reduce falls
Pain plan and safe use of acetaminophen including maximum daily dose and avoiding excess alcohol/other acetaminophen-containing products
Red flags requiring urgent evaluation: sudden rapid worsening, dark urine/marked muscle pain (possible rhabdomyolysis), trouble swallowing, shortness of breath, frequent falls with injury
Discuss family implications and offer genetic counseling/testing options
Set expectations about progression and support resources
Encourage regular low-impact activity within tolerance; avoid extreme eccentric exercise that triggers prolonged weakness
Fall prevention strategies and footwear/home modifications
Importance of managing comorbidities (thyroid disease, diabetes, B12 deficiency) even if not the primary cause

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history: distal weakness pattern, onset/progression, functional impact (falls, foot drop), family history, exposures/meds, and screen for urgent red flags (dysphagia, dyspnea, dark urine, rapid progression).
Order basic evaluation to rule out common mimics/comorbid contributors and establish baseline (e.g., CK, CMP/AST/ALT, TSH, HbA1c/glucose, B12, CBC; consider UA if myoglobinuria concern).
Review labs for patterns consistent with chronic inherited myopathy (often normal/mild CK) and absence of alternative etiologies.
Provide working diagnosis of distal myopathy (Tateyama type) based on phenotype and evaluation; note need for specialist confirmation and genetic testing.
Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment/rehab focus, lifestyle guidance, family implications, and red flags/return precautions.
Critical safety check before any prescribing (e.g., analgesics).
Critical safety check before prescribing (e.g., liver disease/alcohol use for acetaminophen; renal/hepatic issues; other patient-specific contraindications).
Non-critical safety check before prescribing to avoid interactions (e.g., other acetaminophen-containing products; hepatotoxic meds).
If symptomatic pain present, prescribe/advise safe analgesia plan (typically acetaminophen) with dosing limits and avoidance of duplicate products/alcohol; otherwise may omit prescribing.
Arrange neuromuscular specialist referral, PT/OT, orthotics (AFO), and follow-up to review genetic testing/counseling and functional status.
Close after confirming understanding, questions, and safety net instructions.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX’s mother speaking for your 3-year-old son (he answers only simple yes/no). You are anxious and vague. Start with: “He’s losing his vision and keeps stumbling like he can’t control his body.” Volunteer: vision worsening over ~1 year, much worse at night/dim rooms; clumsy “like walking drunk,” frequent tripping/falls; legs seem weaker.

If asked, add: bumps into things/doesn’t see to the side; bright lights bother him and slow light–dark adjustment; occasional slurred-sounding speech when tired; mild hand tremor with fine tasks; cramps/twitches; brief “zones out” episodes.

Do not mention unless specifically asked: easy fatigue, attention/memory issues, muscle loss, intermittent tingling in feet, trouble hearing in noisy places.

Resist/avoid unless directly asked gently: choking/coughing with thin liquids, urinary accidents; if asked, admit reluctantly. Ignore erectile/sex-drive questions as not applicable for a child.

medications: Insulin glargine 3 units subcutaneous nightly
Insulin lispro 0.5-1 unit subcutaneous with meals (carbohydrate-based dosing)
Levetiracetam 150 mg orally twice daily
Polyethylene glycol 3350 8.5 g orally once daily as needed for constipation
confounders: Retinitis pigmentosa from comorbid syndrome contributing to progressive vision loss, complicating attribution of blindness
Diabetic retinopathy risk and fluctuating vision due to hyperglycemia (rare at this age but possible with early-onset diabetes), complicating ocular symptom interpretation
Recurrent aspiration/viral respiratory infections causing intermittent tachypnea and fatigue, mimicking primary pulmonary disease
Antiepileptic adverse effects (somnolence/ataxia) overlapping with neurodegenerative progression
Poor nutritional intake/failure to thrive affecting muscle mass and motor performance independent of neurodegeneration

correct_diagnosis.primary: Sandhoff disease (GM2 gangliosidosis type II due to HEXB deficiency)
acceptable_alternatives: Sandhoff disease, infantile/juvenile form (depending on age of onset)
required_evidence: Markedly reduced total beta-hexosaminidase activity (A+B) in leukocytes
Reduced hexosaminidase A activity in leukocytes (with proportionally reduced total activity, consistent with HEXB deficiency rather than isolated HEXA deficiency)
Biallelic pathogenic variants on HEXB gene testing
Elevated GM2 ganglioside level (plasma) supporting GM2 storage disorder
Clinical phenotype consistent with neurodegeneration and vision loss (blindness)

expected_lab_results.Hexosaminidase A activity (leukocytes): Low (typically reduced; may be low proportionally to total activity rather than isolated deficiency)
expected_lab_results.Total beta-hexosaminidase activity (A+B): Markedly low/near-absent, consistent with Sandhoff disease (HEXB deficiency)
expected_lab_results.HEXB gene testing: Two pathogenic/likely pathogenic variants in HEXB (biallelic); or one pathogenic variant plus a second pathogenic variant/large deletion if included in assay
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Usually normal; may show mild anemia if advanced disease/malnutrition
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive metabolic panel - AST: Normal to mildly elevated (nonspecific; may be elevated if hepatic involvement/illness)

Levetiracetam 10 mg/kg PO BID (if seizures present; titrate to 20-30 mg/kg BID as needed/tolerated)
non_pharmacological: No curative therapy; initiate multidisciplinary supportive care (neurology, genetics/metabolic specialist, ophthalmology, palliative care, PT/OT/SLP)
Vision support and safety planning for blindness (low-vision services, home safety modifications, orientation/mobility training as age-appropriate)
Swallow evaluation and nutrition support; consider feeding tube if aspiration risk or failure to thrive
Respiratory care planning (airway clearance strategies, aspiration precautions, immunizations up to date)

Discuss clinical trials/experimental therapies and referral to specialized centers when appropriate

must_include: Sandhoff disease is an inherited (autosomal recessive) lysosomal storage disorder causing GM2 ganglioside accumulation
Enzyme testing and HEXB genetic testing confirm the diagnosis

There is currently no established curative therapy; care focuses on symptom management and quality of life
Supportive, multidisciplinary management plan (neurology/genetics/ophthalmology/therapy/palliative care)

Discuss referral to specialized centers and potential eligibility for clinical trials

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit presenting concerns, timeline, phenotype clues (infantile vs juvenile/adult), functional status, and caregiver goals; screen for red flags (respiratory distress, aspiration, uncontrolled seizures).

Targeted history: developmental regression, seizures, vision/hearing changes, feeding/swallowing, recurrent infections, sleep, spasticity/ataxia, prior evaluations, newborn screening (if applicable), and family history/consanguinity.

Order/confirm diagnostic testing appropriate for suspected/known Sandhoff: Hexosaminidase A and total hexosaminidase activity, and/or confirmatory HEXB genetic testing; consider baseline labs relevant to supportive care (nutrition/respiratory risk) if clinically indicated.
Review results to confirm Sandhoff disease (low Hex A with low total hex activity consistent with HEXB deficiency and/or pathogenic HEXB variants).
State diagnosis clearly and link it to clinical course/phenotype; document diagnostic confirmation basis (enzyme and/or genetic testing).

Explain diagnosis in plain language including inheritance, pathophysiology (GM2 accumulation), confirmation tests, neurodegenerative course including blindness, and lack of curative therapy; introduce supportive goals.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (CTEPH)
acceptable_alternatives: Chronic thromboembolic pulmonary disease (CTEPD) with pulmonary hypertension
Pulmonary hypertension due to chronic thromboembolic disease
required_evidence: Symptoms/signs consistent with pulmonary hypertension (e.g., progressive exertional dyspnea, reduced exercise tolerance, fatigue, possible syncope, signs of right heart strain)

Evidence of chronic thromboembolic obstruction on imaging (e.g., V/Q scan with mismatched segmental perfusion defects; CT pulmonary angiography showing chronic webs/bands/stenoses/occlusions) after at least 3 months of effective anticoagulation

Hemodynamics confirming pulmonary hypertension by right heart catheterization (mean pulmonary artery pressure >20 mmHg with pulmonary vascular resistance >2 Wood units and pulmonary capillary wedge pressure ≤15 mmHg)
Exclusion of alternative explanations for dyspnea and infiltrates (e.g., COP/AIP/RA-ILD) via clinical course, imaging pattern, and absence of primary parenchymal process explaining PH

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Often normal; may be mildly elevated if chronic hypoxemia (secondary erythrocytosis) or mildly low if chronic disease/anticoagulation-related bleeding—labs are not diagnostic
expected_lab_results.Hematocrit: Often normal; may be mildly elevated with chronic hypoxemia
expected_lab_results.WBC: Typically normal (no primary infectious pattern expected)
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Typically normal; important baseline prior to anticoagulation and to monitor for heparin-induced thrombocytopenia if heparin exposure
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Usually normal; may be low in advanced right heart failure (dilutional hyponatremia)
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Usually normal; may be low/high depending on diuretic use or renal perfusion in right heart failure
expected_lab_results.Chloride: Usually normal; may track with diuretic-related changes
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): Often normal; may be elevated if chronic hypercapnia or diuretic-related metabolic alkalosis

medications: Apixaban 10 mg PO BID for 7 days, then 5 mg PO BID (long-term anticoagulation if no contraindications)
Furosemide 20-40 mg PO daily (if volume overload/right heart failure; titrate to symptoms and electrolytes)
Riociguat 1 mg PO TID, titrate every 2 weeks as tolerated up to 2.5 mg PO TID (for inoperable CTEPH or persistent/recurrent PH after surgery; avoid with PDE-5 inhibitors)
non_pharmacological: Refer to a specialized CTEPH center for operability assessment for pulmonary endarterectomy (PEA) and/or balloon pulmonary angioplasty (BPA)
Oxygen therapy to maintain SpO2 ≥90% if hypoxemic
Supervised pulmonary rehabilitation/exercise training as tolerated
Vaccinations per cardiopulmonary disease guidance (influenza, pneumococcal) and smoking cessation if applicable
Evaluate and manage contributing factors: screen for chronic thromboembolic risk factors, assess for right heart failure, consider sleep-disordered breathing evaluation if suspected
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: CTEPH center referral urgently (within 2-4 weeks); clinical follow-up in 2-4 weeks after starting/adjusting therapy, then every 3 months (or sooner if worsening symptoms)

must_include: CTEPH is high blood pressure in the lungs caused by old, organized blood clots that obstruct pulmonary arteries
This is different from an acute PE and typically requires long-term anticoagulation and specialist evaluation for definitive procedures (PEA/BPA)
Why key tests are needed: V/Q scan and right heart catheterization to confirm and guide treatment
Anticoagulation is long-term to prevent new clots; review bleeding risks and warning signs (melena, hematemesis, severe bruising, hematuria, severe headache/neurologic symptoms)
Definitive options: pulmonary endarterectomy if operable; balloon pulmonary angioplasty if not operable or residual disease
Riociguat role (if inoperable or persistent PH) and key safety point: do not combine with PDE-5 inhibitors; monitor blood pressure and dizziness
Iodinated contrast allergy history: prior mild reaction (hives/pruritus); discuss need for contrast studies, premedication options, and emergency precautions if CT angiography is required
Penicillin allergy: urticarial rash; clarify reaction history and avoid penicillins unless formal allergy evaluation indicates otherwise
Shared decision-making about procedural referral and medication choices based on risks, benefits, and patient goals
Activity guidance: gradual, symptom-limited exercise; avoid overexertion and recognize red flags (syncope, chest pain, worsening dyspnea)
Avoid estrogen-containing therapies if possible due to thrombosis risk; discuss contraception/pregnancy risks in pulmonary hypertension if relevant
Adherence and monitoring: keep follow-up appointments, report bleeding, and avoid NSAIDs/excess alcohol that increase bleeding risk while anticoagulated

patient.instructions:

Volunteer: upper middle belly pain that can go through to your back, vomiting and can’t keep food down, worse after greasy/heavy foods, you’ve been told it was pancreatitis before, and you feel wiped out for days.

Do not mention unless specifically prompted: cloudy/pink ring around colored part of eyes, tingling/burning hands/feet, fear of eating/weight instability, family history, and that even small fat triggers you.

Resist discussing greasy pale foul stools, social avoidance, or OTC “fat blockers” unless the clinician normalizes it.

Express misconceptions: think puffy lips/face might be hereditary swelling; watery diarrhea could be a bug/hormones/tumor; tingling/cramps might be calcium. Assume gene therapy would be covered; avoid insurance/work impact unless pressed; say very-low-fat diets feel too expensive.

If empathy is shown, soften and share one extra detail but stay guarded.

medications: Alipogene tiparvovec (Glybera) one-time gene therapy (historical administration)
Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) 4 g PO daily
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO q6h PRN pain/fever
comorbidities: Nonalcoholic fatty liver disease (mild hepatic steatosis on prior ultrasound)
confounders: Recent alcohol intake (binge within last 72 hours) increasing pancreatitis risk and triglycerides
Recent initiation of combined oral contraceptive pill (estrogen-containing) worsening hypertriglyceridemia
Pseudohyponatremia due to severe lipemia (artifactually low sodium)
Non-fasting sample possibility if patient ate within 8 hours (can further elevate triglycerides)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Familial lipoprotein lipase deficiency (familial chylomicronemia syndrome)
acceptable_alternatives: Familial chylomicronemia syndrome due to LPL pathway defect (e.g., APOC2, APOA5, LMF1, GPIHBP1 deficiency)
required_evidence: Markedly elevated fasting triglycerides consistent with chylomicronemia (typically > 1000 mg/dL and often > 2000 mg/dL) on repeated testing
Milky/lipemic serum appearance (chylomicrons present); may show creamy supernatant on standing
Low or normal apolipoprotein B (supports chylomicronemia rather than apoB-containing particle excess such as familial combined hyperlipidemia)
LDL-C often low/normal by direct measurement; calculated LDL-C unreliable when TG very high

Clinical context compatible with lifelong/severe hypertriglyceridemia and pancreatitis risk; secondary causes assessed/excluded (uncontrolled diabetes, alcohol, hypothyroidism, pregnancy, TG-raising drugs)

expected_lab_results.Triglycerides (fasting): Severely elevated, typically > 1000 mg/dL (11.3 mmol/L), often > 2000 mg/dL; persistent on repeat testing
expected_lab_results.Total cholesterol: Normal to moderately elevated; disproportionately lower than triglycerides
expected_lab_results.LDL-C (direct): Low to normal; may be low because chylomicrons predominate
expected_lab_results.Non-HDL cholesterol: Not markedly elevated relative to triglycerides; may be modestly elevated
expected_lab_results.Apolipoprotein B: Low or low-normal (often < 75 mg/dL), supporting low apoB particle number
expected_lab_results.Serum appearance: Lipemic/milky; may form a creamy layer after refrigeration/standing
expected_lab_results.Glucose (fasting): Often normal; if elevated, evaluate for secondary hypertriglyceridemia contribution

medications: Alipogene tiparvovec 1e12 genome copies/kg intramuscular as a one-time administration per protocol at a specialized center
non_pharmacological: Very-low-fat diet: target < 10–15% of total calories from fat (often 20–30 g/day), avoid large fat boluses
Strict avoidance of alcohol
Avoid refined/simple carbohydrates that can worsen hypertriglyceridemia; emphasize complex carbohydrates and adequate protein
Use medium-chain triglycerides (MCT) as needed for calories because they bypass chylomicron formation
Review and avoid triglyceride-raising medications when possible (e.g., estrogens, isotretinoin, certain antipsychotics, thiazides, beta-blockers, protease inhibitors)
Pancreatitis risk mitigation: educate on early symptoms (severe epigastric pain radiating to back, nausea/vomiting) and urgent evaluation
Genetic counseling and family screening for LPL-pathway disorders
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck fasting triglycerides and clinical status in 4–12 weeks after interventions (or per gene-therapy protocol), then every 3–6 months; immediate follow-up sooner if abdominal pain or pancreatitis symptoms occur

must_include: This condition is an inherited problem in breaking down chylomicrons due to LPL-pathway dysfunction, causing very high triglycerides
Main medical risk is recurrent pancreatitis; cardiovascular risk assessment differs from common high-apoB dyslipidemias
Why key lab features fit (very high fasting TG, milky serum, low/normal apoB, low/normal LDL-C)
Primary management is strict dietary fat restriction and avoidance of triggers; medications like fibrates/omega-3 often have limited benefit in true LPL deficiency
Alipogene tiparvovec is a specialized one-time gene therapy given at expert centers; discuss realistic goals (TG reduction and pancreatitis risk reduction) and monitoring requirements
When to seek urgent care for pancreatitis symptoms
Discuss heritable nature and implications for family members
Set expectations about chronic lifestyle requirements even with advanced therapies
Review allergy history (amoxicillin-clavulanate rash) and how allergies are checked before any new treatment
Concrete diet targets (very-low-fat grams/day or percent calories) and strategies (label reading, meal planning, avoiding high-fat foods)
Alcohol avoidance and minimizing simple sugars
Use of MCT to maintain nutrition while limiting long-chain fats
Importance of fasting labs and adherence to reduce pancreatitis risk

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for severe hypertriglyceridemia syndromes (pancreatitis symptoms/history, diet/alcohol, secondary causes, family history, pregnancy/estrogen exposure, diabetes/thyroid/renal/liver disease, medication triggers) and confirm allergy history.

Order confirmatory and secondary-cause labs for suspected familial chylomicronemia syndrome (fasting lipid panel with TG, apoB, CMP/LFTs, A1c/glucose, TSH, CBC; consider amylase/lipase if symptomatic; urinalysis/pregnancy test when relevant).
Review results for classic pattern (very high fasting TG, lipemic/milky serum, low/normal apoB, low/normal LDL-C) and exclude secondary causes that can mimic or worsen chylomicronemia.

Make and communicate diagnosis of familial lipoprotein lipase (LPL-pathway) deficiency / familial chylomicronemia syndrome with risk framing (recurrent pancreatitis primary risk; ASCVD risk differs from apoB-driven dyslipidemias).

Provide required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment (diet-first; limited benefit of fibrates/omega-3 in true LPL deficiency; gene therapy option at expert centers), urgent pancreatitis warning signs, heritability/family implications, and concrete lifestyle plan (very-low-fat diet targets, alcohol avoidance, minimize simple sugars, MCT use, fasting labs/adherence).
Arrange close follow-up for diet implementation, repeat fasting TG monitoring, pancreatitis risk review, and referral to lipid specialist/genetic counseling/expert center if considering gene therapy.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-net instructions, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 47-year-old anxious grocery shift supervisor. You speak tangentially, jumping between symptoms, work stress, and costs. Start with: burning when you pee for about a week that won’t go away. Volunteer: peeing a lot with little output, strong-smelling urine. If asked, add urgency (barely make it), dull low belly ache, cloudy urine, no fever, no flank/rib back pain, and you’ve had this once or twice before.

Only reveal later if specifically probed: you’ve been drinking less water because it hurts, using a new scented body wash “down there,” you hold your pee at work, and you’ve been constipated.

Express misconceptions: you’re sure it’s “just a UTI,” stress causes it, you took 2 days of a family member’s leftover antibiotics (helped then returned), and cranberry pills should fix it.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Data insufficient to determine a single primary diagnosis (requires symptom history, vitals, exam, and additional labs/imaging).
required_evidence: Chief complaint and symptom timeline (fever, pain location, cough, dysuria, GI symptoms, rash, bleeding, fatigue, weight loss)
Vital signs (temperature, heart rate, blood pressure, respiratory rate, oxygen saturation)
Focused physical exam (cardiopulmonary, abdominal, skin, neuro, hydration status)
Medication list and recent exposures (antibiotics, NSAIDs, diuretics, ACEi/ARB, supplements)
Renal function (creatinine/eGFR) and glucose (not provided but required for BUN interpretation and metabolic assessment)
Urinalysis +/- urine culture if urinary symptoms or AKI suspected
Pregnancy status when applicable
Additional targeted testing based on symptoms (CXR, ECG, troponin, LFTs, lactate, cultures)

expected_lab_results.WBC: Indeterminate without a defined illness; may be elevated in bacterial infection, low/normal in viral infection, or abnormal in hematologic disease.
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Indeterminate; may be low with anemia/bleeding/chronic disease or normal in acute infection/dehydration.
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Indeterminate; may be low in sepsis/ITP/drug-induced thrombocytopenia or high in inflammation/iron deficiency.
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Indeterminate; may be low with hypovolemia/SIADH or high with dehydration.
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Indeterminate; may be high with renal impairment/acidosis or low with GI losses/diuretics.
expected_lab_results.Chloride: Indeterminate; may track sodium or be high in non-anion-gap metabolic acidosis.
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): Indeterminate; may be low in metabolic acidosis (sepsis, renal failure, diarrhea) or high in metabolic alkalosis (vomiting, diuretics).
expected_lab_results.BUN: Indeterminate; may be elevated with dehydration/pre-renal azotemia, GI bleed, or renal impairment.

medications: No empiric medications can be safely specified without diagnosis, renal function (creatinine/eGFR), vitals, and symptom context; provide supportive care only until assessment is completed.
non_pharmacological: Immediate clinical assessment: obtain full history, vitals, and focused exam

If unstable (hypotension, hypoxia, altered mental status, severe pain, concern for sepsis): activate emergency evaluation, establish IV access, begin isotonic fluids as indicated, and obtain cultures/imaging guided by presentation
Ensure adequate oral hydration if stable and able to tolerate; avoid alcohol and nephrotoxins (e.g., NSAIDs) until renal status clarified
Arrange appropriate diagnostic workup based on symptoms (e.g., urinalysis, creatinine/eGFR, glucose, lactate, cultures, CXR)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Same day urgent evaluation (ED/urgent care) if any red flags or abnormal vitals; otherwise within 24-48 hours after completing missing diagnostic data.

What tests will guide treatment decisions (e.g., creatinine/eGFR, urinalysis, imaging)
Penicillin allergy history (urticarial rash) and implications for antibiotic selection if needed

Red-flag symptoms requiring immediate care (worsening shortness of breath, chest pain, confusion, fainting, persistent high fever, severe weakness, inability to keep fluids down, decreased urination, signs of bleeding)
Shared decision-making about level of care (clinic vs urgent care vs ED) based on symptoms and vitals
Hydration guidance and when to limit/seek care
Avoid NSAIDs and other nephrotoxic agents until kidney function clarified

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit chief concern, symptom timeline, severity, and context because disease is unknown and available information is incomplete.
Assess stability and red flags (vitals if available, mental status, chest pain, dyspnea, syncope, dehydration, urine output, bleeding) to determine level of care (clinic vs urgent care vs ED).

Targeted review for likely sources: infectious (fever, cough, dysuria), cardiopulmonary, GI losses, medication/toxin exposure, pregnancy status when relevant, and recent procedures/travel/sick contacts.

Order basic labs needed to interpret any abnormal results safely: BMP/CMP including creatinine/eGFR, electrolytes, glucose; CBC; urinalysis with microscopy; urine culture if urinary symptoms; pregnancy test if applicable.

Order condition-guiding tests based on symptoms: lactate and blood cultures if sepsis concern; LFTs if jaundice; CK if rhabdomyolysis risk; BNP/troponin/ECG if chest pain/SOB; CXR if respiratory symptoms; renal ultrasound if obstruction suspected.
Review returned results to stratify urgency (AKI, severe electrolyte abnormalities, anemia/bleeding, infection markers) and guide next steps without premature diagnosis.
Provide a differential diagnosis rather than a single label if data remain insufficient; state what is known/unknown and what additional data are needed.
Communicate diagnosis uncertainty, what labs can/cannot show without symptoms/vitals/creatinine, and outline the next questions/tests and immediate safety plan.
Explain supportive care plan (hydration guidance, avoid nephrotoxins/NSAIDs) and criteria for urgent evaluation; avoid empiric antibiotics unless unstable or sepsis suspected.
Before any prescribing, confirm allergy history (penicillin urticarial rash) and document implications for antibiotic selection if later needed.
Before any prescribing, evaluate drug-drug interactions with current meds/OTCs/supplements.
Confirm contraindications relevant to any contemplated medication class (renal impairment, pregnancy, QT risk, hepatic disease) prior to prescribing.

Only prescribe if there is a clear indication and safety checks are satisfied; prefer supportive/low-risk therapies (e.g., oral rehydration, acetaminophen) and avoid condition-specific drugs without an established source/diagnosis unless unstable.
Arrange follow-up based on risk (same day/24–48h for abnormal labs or persistent symptoms; routine if reassuring) and ensure clear return precautions.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, shared decision on level of care, and documenting safety-net instructions.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a cooperative, clear-speaking parent of a 2-year-old girl. Start by saying: “My baby has had nonstop watery diarrhea and isn’t gaining weight.” Early in the visit, volunteer: watery diarrhea many times daily since early infancy, poor weight gain despite frequent feeding, smaller than peers, tired/weak, swollen belly and lots of gas. If asked about stool details, say it began in the first weeks of life and never fully stopped; stools are very watery and sometimes oily/hard to wipe. If asked, report easy dehydration (dry mouth, fewer wet diapers), more vomiting/spit-up, recurrent ear/chest infections, thin/breaking hair, dry rashy skin, and occasional low-grade fevers.

Do not mention unless specifically asked: delayed milestones, puffiness around eyes/ankles, easy bruising/petechiae, pale gums/shortness of breath with hard crying, and intermittent poor appetite.

Express misconceptions: you suspect milk allergy; formula changes/dairy-free didn’t fix it; a relative mentioned celiac. If cost/feeding details are pressed, admit you sometimes water down formula because it’s expensive. Be skeptical about “premium” formulas, frequent labs, and specialists; ask if visits/tests can be spaced out. If the clinician shows empathy, you become more open and relieved, and answer honestly.

medications: Total parenteral nutrition (TPN) via central line continuous infusion nightly
Ursodeoxycholic acid 10 mg/kg/day PO divided BID
Loperamide 0.1 mg/kg PO TID PRN diarrhea

Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 800 IU PO daily

comorbidities: Chronic watery diarrhea with intestinal failure requiring partial TPN dependence
confounders: TPN-associated cholestasis can mimic or worsen primary liver involvement
Small intestinal bacterial overgrowth related to intestinal dysmotility/TPN can contribute to diarrhea and elevated fecal calprotectin
Cow's milk protein intolerance history with prior elimination diet response partially overlaps with chronic diarrhea presentation
Recent viral gastroenteritis episode (2 weeks ago) complicates assessment of baseline diarrhea and electrolyte losses
Central line-related inflammation/infection risk may intermittently elevate liver enzymes and worsen nutritional status

correct_diagnosis.primary: Tricho-hepato-enteric syndrome (syndromic infantile diarrhea due to TTC37/SKIV2L-related disorder)
acceptable_alternatives: Syndromic congenital diarrhea (genetic enteropathy) consistent with tricho-hepato-enteric syndrome
Congenital diarrheal disorder with hair abnormalities and liver disease (suspected tricho-hepato-enteric syndrome)
required_evidence: Early-onset (infancy) chronic intractable diarrhea with poor weight gain/failure to thrive
Characteristic hair abnormality (woolly/brittle hair, trichorrhexis nodosa) and/or facial dysmorphism
Evidence of liver involvement (hepatomegaly, elevated transaminases/cholestasis) or history consistent with hepatic disease
Exclusion of common acquired causes of chronic diarrhea (infection, cow’s milk protein allergy) and other congenital diarrheal disorders
Confirmation by genetic testing identifying pathogenic variants in TTC37 or SKIV2L (if available)

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Low (anemia of chronic disease and/or nutritional deficiency)
expected_lab_results.MCV: Low to normal (microcytic if iron deficiency; may be normal early)
expected_lab_results.WBC: Normal to mildly elevated (may be normal; consider infection if elevated)
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Normal to elevated (reactive thrombocytosis possible with inflammation/iron deficiency)
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Low to normal (hyponatremia possible with chronic diarrhea/dehydration)
expected_lab_results.Chloride: Normal to low (can be low with diarrhea-related electrolyte loss)
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): Low (non-anion gap metabolic acidosis due to diarrheal bicarbonate loss)

medications: Oral rehydration solution per WHO formula PO ad lib to replace ongoing stool losses
0.9% sodium chloride 20 mL/kg IV bolus once (repeat as needed) for moderate-severe dehydration or poor oral intake
Potassium chloride 0.5-1 mEq/kg/day PO/IV divided q6-12h after urine output confirmed, titrate to labs
Multivitamin with fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) PO daily
Elemental or extensively hydrolyzed formula PO/NG continuous or bolus feeds as tolerated (nutrition therapy)
non_pharmacological: Admit for assessment of hydration, electrolyte correction, and nutritional rehabilitation if failure to thrive or significant electrolyte derangements
Early involvement of pediatric gastroenterology, genetics, nutrition/dietitian; consider immunology/hepatology based on phenotype
Nutritional support escalation as needed: high-calorie feeds, NG feeds, and parenteral nutrition if enteral feeding insufficient
Monitor stool output, daily weights, strict intake/output; repeat electrolytes to guide replacement
Plan for confirmatory genetic testing (TTC37/SKIV2L) and evaluation for associated liver disease and immune dysfunction
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1 week after stabilization/discharge (earlier within 24-72 hours if outpatient electrolyte abnormalities), then frequent multidisciplinary follow-up every 2-4 weeks during nutritional optimization

must_include: This is a rare, inherited condition causing severe chronic diarrhea and poor growth starting in infancy
It can be associated with hair changes and sometimes liver and immune system involvement
Diagnosis often requires genetic testing and specialist evaluation
Main goals: prevent dehydration, correct electrolytes, and support growth with specialized nutrition
Some children need tube feeding and/or parenteral nutrition if they cannot absorb enough through the gut
Ongoing monitoring is needed because electrolyte shifts and malnutrition can recur
Review red flags requiring urgent care: lethargy, decreased urination, persistent vomiting, worsening diarrhea, blood in stool, fever
Discuss expected course: chronic condition with periods of instability; prognosis varies
Explain the need for multidisciplinary care and potential hospitalizations during flares
Hydration plan: how to use oral rehydration solution and when to increase intake based on stool losses
Nutrition plan: importance of prescribed formula/diet and adherence to feeding regimen
Medication safety: avoid amoxicillin-clavulanate due to prior urticarial rash; inform all clinicians of this allergy

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit focused history for chronic infantile diarrhea and failure to thrive; characterize stool losses, hydration status, feeding tolerance, growth trajectory, prior evaluations, infections, and family history/consanguinity; confirm medication allergies (notably amoxicillin-clavulanate urticaria).

Order baseline evaluation to assess dehydration/electrolyte derangements and organ involvement: BMP/electrolytes, glucose, Mg/Phos, CBC, LFTs, albumin/prealbumin, stool studies as appropriate, and nutritional markers; consider immunologic screening if clinically indicated.

Interpret results to identify urgent abnormalities (hypo-/hypernatremia, metabolic acidosis, hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, hypoalbuminemia) and evidence of liver involvement or infection; determine need for escalation.
State suspected/confirmed tricho-hepato-enteric syndrome and explain rationale; note that confirmation typically requires genetic testing and specialist evaluation.

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment goals (hydration/electrolytes/nutrition, possible tube feeds/TPN), address concerns/red flags/prognosis variability/multidisciplinary care, and lifestyle education including ORS plan, nutrition adherence, and medication allergy safety.
Before any prescribing, verify and document allergies (critical safety check), specifically prior urticarial rash to amoxicillin-clavulanate.
If prescribing is needed, screen for drug-drug interactions with current meds/supplements/TPN additives (non-critical but expected when applicable).

If clinically indicated, prescribe supportive therapies consistent with findings (e.g., oral rehydration solution regimen, electrolyte supplementation, vitamins/minerals) avoiding contraindicated/allergenic agents; ensure dosing appropriate for age/weight and clinical status.
Arrange close follow-up and referrals (pediatric gastroenterology, genetics, nutrition, possibly immunology/hepatology); plan monitoring of growth and labs; provide clear return precautions.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX’s anxious parent. Speak vaguely at first, focused on: “He gets fevers out of nowhere every few weeks, usually evenings, lasts 2–4 days, then he’s wiped out and pale for days.” Volunteer that he’s had lots of infections (ear/sinus), many antibiotics this year, and he’s not growing/keeping up at school.

If asked, add: mouth sores and sore throat during fevers, belly pain with occasional loose stools, swollen neck glands, gets short of breath faster playing, bruises easily and has frequent nosebleeds, and he says his heart “races” with fever.

Do not mention unless specifically asked about anemia, diet, jaundice, dizziness, or development: long history of anemia since infancy, iron pills didn’t help, chews ice, poor appetite, looks a bit yellow after illnesses, headaches/standing dizziness, slow speech and trouble with multi-step instructions.

Be hesitant to bring up genital-area rash/burning with urination after antibiotics and missed school/special-ed talk; only admit if directly asked, embarrassed.

Express misconceptions: think it’s recurring flu; ask about Lyme or food allergies; worry it’s a heart problem.

When clinicians show empathy, soften, thank them, and share more. Push back on costs; ask to space tests, skip genetics/immune workup, and stop preventive meds when he seems well.

Ferrous sulfate 0 mg (not prescribed; iron avoided due to overload risk)
Acetaminophen 15 mg/kg PO every 6 hours as needed for fever
Deferasirox 20 mg/kg PO daily
comorbidities: Iron overload secondary to chronic transfusions

Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole may cause cytopenias or rash, complicating infection vs drug reaction evaluation
IVIG can transiently alter serologic testing and vaccine antibody titers

general_concerns: Believes Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia is 'basically the same as multiple myeloma,' so he assumes he will inevitably need a stem cell transplant soon.
Thinks ibrutinib works like traditional chemotherapy and expects severe nausea/hair loss; worries that feeling tired means the drug is 'poisoning' him.
Assumes if his blood tests improve, he can stop ibrutinib for a while to 'give the body a break' without consequences.
Believes he should avoid all vaccinations because they might 'stimulate the cancer' or be unsafe while on ibrutinib.
Thinks bruising or mild nosebleeds are harmless and not worth reporting because they are 'just part of getting older.'
Assumes any new shortness of breath or palpitations are anxiety-related and not potentially related to medication side effects.
economic_concerns: Thinks using a manufacturer copay card is 'insurance fraud' and avoids asking about assistance programs.
Plans to stretch his ibrutinib supply by taking it every other day to reduce cost, assuming it will work almost as well.
Worries that frequent lab monitoring and follow-up visits will lead to unexpected bills, so he considers skipping appointments when he feels okay.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 72-year-old retired high school math teacher (clear, cooperative). Start by saying: “I’ve been feeling wiped out for months, and lately my nose keeps bleeding and my vision gets blurry sometimes.” Early in the visit, volunteer: months of severe fatigue/weakness; nosebleeds harder to stop; intermittent blurry vision when reading/TV; lightheaded on standing. If asked, add: pressure-like headaches, shortness of breath on stairs, 8-lb unintentional weight loss, night sweats, mild gum bleeding with brushing, tingling/numbness in hands/feet. Do NOT mention unless specifically asked: fingers turning pale/blue and painful in cold; brief episodes of confusion/fogginess; easy bruising. Be reluctant/embarrassed about new erectile dysfunction—only disclose if asked directly and with reassurance. Express misconceptions: think glasses/stress explain symptoms; wonder tingling is “pre-diabetes.” If empathy is shown, you relax, answer fully, and admit money worries: you fear copay cards are “insurance fraud,” consider taking ibrutinib every other day to stretch supply, and might skip labs/visits to avoid bills.

medications: Ibrutinib 420 mg PO daily
Amlodipine 5 mg PO daily
Atorvastatin 20 mg PO nightly
Apixaban 5 mg PO twice daily
Acyclovir 400 mg PO twice daily
Pantoprazole 40 mg PO daily
Ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO every other day
confounders: Atrial fibrillation on apixaban with concurrent ibrutinib increases bleeding risk and can confound evaluation of mucosal bleeding/bruising
Chronic kidney disease can contribute to anemia and neuropathy symptoms, complicating attribution to Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia
Iron deficiency from occult gastrointestinal blood loss (anticoagulation-related) may coexist with anemia from marrow infiltration
Peripheral neuropathy could be due to IgM anti-myelin-associated glycoprotein activity versus age-related/compressive neuropathy
Hyperviscosity symptoms (headache, blurred vision) may be mistaken for uncontrolled hypertension or medication effects

correct_diagnosis.primary: Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia (lymphoplasmacytic lymphoma with IgM monoclonal gammopathy)
acceptable_alternatives: Lymphoplasmacytic lymphoma with IgM monoclonal gammopathy
Waldenström macroglobulinemia on ibrutinib therapy (follow-up/monitoring visit)
required_evidence: Known diagnosis of Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia and currently treated with ibrutinib
Evidence of cytopenias consistent with marrow involvement (e.g., anemia and/or thrombocytopenia) on CBC
Renal function assessment to guide supportive care and dosing considerations (creatinine/eGFR available)
Reticulocyte count to help characterize anemia (often low/normal retic in marrow infiltration)

expected_lab_results.MCV: Usually normal (normocytic) but may be mildly elevated/variable depending on comorbid factors
expected_lab_results.WBC: Normal or mildly low (possible leukopenia from marrow involvement or therapy)
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Normal or low (thrombocytopenia possible from marrow involvement)
expected_lab_results.Reticulocyte count: Low or inappropriately normal for degree of anemia (hypoproliferative pattern)
expected_lab_results.Creatinine: Usually normal; may be elevated if renal impairment/comorbidity present
expected_lab_results.eGFR: Usually preserved; may be reduced if renal impairment present

medications: Ibrutinib 420 mg PO once daily (continue if tolerated and no contraindications; do not stop abruptly without hematology guidance)
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain/fever (max 3000 mg/day if older/frail or liver risk; otherwise max 4000 mg/day)
non_pharmacological: Monitor CBC and renal function regularly to assess response/toxicity (anemia, thrombocytopenia, leukopenia)
Bleeding-risk precautions while on ibrutinib (avoid NSAIDs if possible; minimize trauma; report easy bruising/bleeding)
Infection precautions and prompt evaluation for fever (given potential leukopenia and BTK-inhibitor associated infection risk)
Vaccinations per immunocompromised guidance (annual inactivated influenza; pneumococcal; avoid live vaccines while on therapy)
Coordinate with hematology/oncology for ongoing disease monitoring and consideration of therapy adjustment if cytopenias worsen
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck CBC (Hgb/Hct/MCV/WBC/platelets) and creatinine/eGFR with reticulocyte count in 2-4 weeks, sooner (within 1 week) if symptomatic anemia, bleeding, infection symptoms, or rapid lab decline; routine hematology follow-up per treatment protocol (often every 4-12 weeks once stable).

must_include: Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia is a slow-growing blood cancer (lymphoplasmacytic lymphoma) that makes an abnormal IgM antibody
It can cause anemia/low blood counts due to bone marrow involvement
Goals of care are symptom control and preventing complications; many patients need long-term monitoring
Ibrutinib is a BTK inhibitor used to control the disease and improve blood counts/symptoms over time
How to take it (once daily, consistent schedule) and not to stop without contacting oncology
Key adverse effects to watch for: bleeding/bruising, atrial fibrillation/palpitations, infections/fever, diarrhea, rash
Need for periodic labs (CBC and kidney function) to monitor response and side effects
Review allergy history (penicillin causes urticarial rash; TMP-SMX causes nausea and pruritic rash) before any new medications
Discuss bleeding risk and what to do if black stools, prolonged bleeding, severe bruising, or severe headache occurs
Provide clear return precautions for fever (e.g., ≥38.0°C/100.4°F), chest pain, shortness of breath, palpitations, or syncope
Avoid NSAIDs and limit alcohol if bleeding risk is a concern; use acetaminophen preferentially unless contraindicated
Avoid grapefruit/Seville oranges (CYP3A interaction risk with ibrutinib) and check with clinician before starting new OTC meds/supplements
Hand hygiene and infection prevention; keep vaccinations up to date with non-live vaccines

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit current symptoms and red flags relevant to Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia and ibrutinib initiation (anemia symptoms, bleeding, infection, hyperviscosity/neurologic symptoms, cardiac history), plus current meds/OTCs/supplements and alcohol use.
Perform required critical allergy check before any prescribing; confirm reported penicillin urticarial rash and TMP-SMX nausea/pruritic rash and ask about reaction severity/anaphylaxis.

Perform required critical drug interaction check before prescribing ibrutinib; screen for CYP3A inhibitors/inducers, anticoagulants/antiplatelets, NSAIDs, and herbal supplements (e.g., St John’s wort).
Specifically assess food/supplement interactions relevant to ibrutinib (grapefruit/Seville oranges) and OTC additions; reinforce need to consult before starting new agents.

Perform required critical contraindication check before prescribing; assess pregnancy status if relevant, severe hepatic impairment, active major bleeding, uncontrolled arrhythmia, planned surgery/procedures, and uncontrolled infection.

Order baseline monitoring labs for therapy and disease assessment: CBC with differential/platelets and kidney function (BMP/creatinine) per milestone; may include LFTs if local protocol, but at minimum CBC and renal function.
Review baseline labs to ensure it is safe to start/continue ibrutinib and to establish monitoring baseline (cytopenias, renal function).
State/confirm Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia diagnosis and briefly connect symptoms/labs to marrow involvement and IgM-related complications; emphasize chronic/indolent course and monitoring goals.

Prescribe ibrutinib only after completing allergy, interaction, and contraindication checks; include dosing schedule guidance (once daily, consistent time) and instructions not to stop without oncology input.

Provide required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment purpose, key adverse effects (bleeding, AF/palpitations, infection/fever, diarrhea, rash), lab monitoring, bleeding precautions, drug/food interactions, and infection prevention/vaccines.

Arrange follow-up for lab monitoring and symptom review; ensure clear return precautions (fever ≥38.0°C/100.4°F, chest pain, SOB, palpitations/syncope, black stools, severe headache, prolonged bleeding).
Close encounter after confirming understanding, addressing questions, and documenting safety-net instructions.
