correct_diagnosis.primary: Sialuria (GNE-related sialic acid storage disorder; autosomal dominant)
acceptable_alternatives: GNE sialuria (autosomal dominant free sialic aciduria)
Sialic acid storage disorder due to feedback-resistant UDP-GlcNAc 2-epimerase (GNE)
required_evidence: Markedly increased urine free sialic acid on quantitative testing
Increased plasma free sialic acid (supportive)
Free sialic acid disproportionately elevated compared with total sialic acid (pattern consistent with sialuria)
Exclude common acquired causes of glycosuria/ketonuria (urinalysis typically negative for glucose/ketones in isolated sialuria)

expected_lab_results.Urine total sialic acid (quantitative): Normal to mildly elevated; increase less pronounced than free fraction

medications: No disease-specific pharmacotherapy indicated; avoid empiric antibiotics unless clear evidence of infection
non_pharmacological: Refer to metabolic genetics for confirmatory testing (GNE gene) and longitudinal management
Genetic counseling (autosomal dominant inheritance; discuss family screening/testing)
Supportive care based on symptoms (e.g., manage intercurrent infections per standard practice; ensure routine preventive care)
Provide education that condition is typically benign/slowly progressive; monitor for rare complications as directed by genetics specialist
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Metabolic genetics appointment within 4-8 weeks; sooner (1-2 weeks) if significant symptoms or diagnostic uncertainty

must_include: Sialuria is a rare inherited metabolic condition with excess free sialic acid in urine (and often blood)
Diagnosis is supported by the specific lab pattern (free fraction markedly elevated)
Inheritance is typically autosomal dominant; family members may benefit from counseling/testing
Condition often has a relatively benign course but needs specialist confirmation and monitoring
No proven curative medication; management is mainly supportive and specialist-led
Plan for confirmatory genetic testing and follow-up with metabolic genetics
Avoid unnecessary medications (e.g., antibiotics) unless clearly indicated
Discuss what symptoms to watch for and when to seek urgent care (e.g., dehydration, persistent vomiting, severe illness)
Offer resources for rare disease support and clarify uncertainties/next steps
Discuss implications for relatives and reproductive planning if relevant
Maintain normal balanced diet unless genetics team advises otherwise
Encourage routine health maintenance and vaccinations
Emphasize keeping follow-up appointments and bringing family history information

Communicate diagnosis, typical inheritance, prognosis, monitoring needs, and next steps; address patient concerns, red flags, family implications, and lifestyle guidance.
Arrange metabolic genetics referral, genetic counseling/testing plan, and follow-up for monitoring and review of results; provide contingency plan for urgent symptoms.
Close with teach-back, confirm understanding, and ensure the patient knows how to access urgent care and follow-up.

Thinks that because her genetic change is a 'point mutation' it is mild and she does not need ongoing specialist follow-up.
Assumes growth hormone treatment is only for making people taller and is unnecessary after the teenage years.
Believes that if she feels hungry all the time, it means her body truly needs more calories and restricting food is unsafe.
Thinks sleepiness and snoring are just part of Prader-Willi and not something that needs evaluation.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 19-year-old woman. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by saying: “I can’t stop feeling hungry, and I’ve been gaining weight no matter what I do.” Volunteer early: you’re hungry all the time even right after eating, weight gain has been fast, and you feel tired during the day.

If asked about childhood or development, share: you were a “floppy baby” with feeding trouble, you’ve always been short with small hands/feet, you rarely feel full, you struggled in school and need help with planning, routine changes trigger big outbursts, puberty felt abnormal and sex drive is low, and you snore loudly and sometimes wake up gasping.

Do not volunteer hidden issues unless specifically asked (sweat less, low pain awareness, constipation, skin picking/sores, episodes of eating so fast your stomach hurt).

Be hesitant/ashamed about secret eating, taking food, or vomiting—only admit if asked directly and reassured.

Mention you suspect thyroid problems from online searching; also wonder if stress/depression explains it. If the clinician shows empathy, you relax, answer more fully, and express worries about costs/coverage, food affordability, and losing support benefits if you work more.

diagnosis.primary: Prader-Willi syndrome due to point mutation

correct_diagnosis.primary: Prader-Willi syndrome (PWS) due to point mutation affecting the 15q11-q13 paternal gene expression region
acceptable_alternatives: Prader-Willi syndrome with endocrine complications (e.g., growth hormone deficiency, hypogonadotropic hypogonadism, obesity-related insulin resistance)
required_evidence: Genetic confirmation consistent with PWS (point mutation affecting paternal expression in 15q11-q13 region)
Clinical phenotype consistent with PWS (hyperphagia/food-seeking with obesity risk, developmental/behavioral features, hypotonia history)
Endocrine screening consistent with common PWS comorbidities (possible low/low-normal IGF-1; possible low LH/FSH for age/sex; metabolic risk on glucose/insulin testing)
Thyroid screening to assess for central hypothyroidism (TSH may be normal/low with low free T4)

expected_lab_results.Fasting plasma glucose: Normal to mildly elevated (may be in prediabetes range depending on obesity/insulin resistance)
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin A1c: Normal to mildly elevated (may indicate prediabetes if metabolic syndrome present)
expected_lab_results.Fasting insulin: Often elevated consistent with insulin resistance (can be normal if lean/young or on strict diet)
expected_lab_results.TSH: Normal or low-normal (may be inappropriately normal if central hypothyroidism)
expected_lab_results.Free T4: Normal or low (low free T4 with non-elevated TSH suggests central hypothyroidism)
expected_lab_results.IGF-1: Low or low-normal for age (suggestive of relative growth hormone deficiency in PWS)
expected_lab_results.LH: Low or inappropriately normal for pubertal status/sex (hypogonadotropic hypogonadism pattern)
expected_lab_results.FSH: Low or inappropriately normal for pubertal status/sex (hypogonadotropic hypogonadism pattern)

medications: Somatropin 0.18-0.30 mg subcutaneous once daily (or ~0.24 mg/kg/week divided daily; titrate to keep IGF-1 within age-adjusted normal range)
Levothyroxine 1.6 mcg/kg/day oral once daily (only if free T4 is low or central hypothyroidism suspected; titrate to free T4 in upper half of normal range)
Metformin 500 mg oral once daily with food, increase every 1-2 weeks as tolerated to 1000 mg oral twice daily (if prediabetes/diabetes or significant insulin resistance is present)
non_pharmacological: Strict environmental food security (locked food access, structured meals/snacks, no unsupervised food access)
Calorie-controlled nutritionally complete diet with dietitian involvement; higher protein/fiber strategies as appropriate
Daily physical activity plan tailored to ability; resistance training to support lean mass
Behavioral supports for food-seeking, tantrums/rigidity; consider referral to psychology/behavior therapy
Screen and manage sleep-disordered breathing prior to and during growth hormone therapy (clinical screening and referral for sleep study if symptoms or risk factors)
Monitor weight/BMI, blood pressure, and metabolic parameters regularly; coordinate multidisciplinary care (endocrinology, nutrition, developmental/behavioral services)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Endocrinology follow-up in 6-12 weeks after initiating or adjusting therapy, then every 3-6 months; metabolic labs at least every 6-12 months (more often if abnormal)

must_include: PWS is a genetic condition affecting appetite regulation, growth/body composition, and hormone function
The subtype here is due to a point mutation affecting the paternal gene expression region on chromosome 15
Common associated issues include hyperphagia/obesity risk and endocrine problems (growth hormone deficiency, hypogonadism, possible central hypothyroidism, glucose intolerance)
Growth hormone therapy can improve body composition, linear growth (if pediatric), strength, and possibly metabolic health; requires monitoring (IGF-1, side effects, sleep/breathing concerns)
Thyroid hormone replacement is used only if labs suggest hypothyroidism; goal is to normalize free T4 rather than relying on TSH in central disease
If glucose intolerance/insulin resistance is present, lifestyle is first-line and metformin may be added; ongoing monitoring of A1c/glucose is needed
Review potential adverse effects and warning signs for growth hormone (worsening snoring/apnea, headaches/vision changes, hip/knee pain/limp, edema)
Discuss that food security measures are a medical necessity in PWS, not a punishment
Confirm amoxicillin allergy (hives) and advise avoiding penicillin-class antibiotics unless evaluated by allergy specialist
Implement structured meal plan with controlled access to food and consistent routines
Encourage daily activity and family/caregiver involvement to maintain adherence
Set expectations for long-term multidisciplinary follow-up and periodic lab monitoring (glucose/A1c, thyroid function, IGF-1, pubertal hormones as relevant)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit age/developmental stage, current concerns (hyperphagia/weight, growth, behavior, sleep), prior genetic testing, current meds/supplements, comorbidities, family support, and goals of visit.
Perform required critical safety check before any prescribing; confirm reported amoxicillin allergy details (e.g., hives) and document.

Screen for contraindications to growth hormone therapy and other common PWS risks: snoring/apnea, daytime somnolence, scoliosis/hip pain, headaches/vision changes, edema, history of malignancy, uncontrolled diabetes, acute respiratory issues.

Order baseline and monitoring labs relevant to PWS endocrine/metabolic issues and proposed treatments: IGF-1 (for GH monitoring), free T4 (± TSH), A1c/fasting glucose (± lipids), and other age-appropriate endocrine labs if indicated.
Use results to determine whether thyroid replacement is indicated (central hypothyroidism pattern) and whether glucose intolerance/diabetes is present for lifestyle vs metformin consideration.
State/confirm Prader-Willi syndrome due to point mutation affecting paternal gene expression region on chromosome 15; connect diagnosis to clinical features and risks.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment options/monitoring, address concerns (GH warning signs; food security as medical necessity; penicillin avoidance with amoxicillin hives), and provide lifestyle plan with multidisciplinary follow-up expectations.
If prescribing is considered, perform non-critical interaction check with current meds/supplements (especially if adding metformin or levothyroxine) to ensure safe co-administration.

Only if indicated and after safety checks: initiate thyroid hormone if free T4 low (central hypothyroidism), consider metformin if glucose intolerance/insulin resistance, and/or document GH therapy plan/referral per local protocols with monitoring and sleep-breathing evaluation as appropriate.
Arrange follow-up for lab review, monitoring (IGF-1, glucose/A1c, free T4), sleep evaluation if symptoms, and multidisciplinary supports (endocrinology, nutrition, behavioral, PT/OT).
Close the encounter after confirming understanding, providing return precautions (GH adverse effects warning signs), and ensuring a concrete follow-up plan.

nl_assertions: The agent explains PWS as a genetic condition affecting appetite regulation, growth/body composition, and hormone function.
The agent specifies that this subtype is due to a point mutation affecting the paternal gene expression region on chromosome 15 (without incorrectly describing deletion or maternal UPD as the cause).

The agent mentions common associated issues: hyperphagia/obesity risk and endocrine problems including growth hormone deficiency, hypogonadism, possible central hypothyroidism, and glucose intolerance.
Before any prescribing, the agent completes and documents an allergy check and a contraindication check (both critical).
The agent confirms the amoxicillin allergy as hives and advises avoiding penicillin-class antibiotics unless evaluated by an allergy specialist.

When discussing growth hormone therapy, the agent states expected benefits (body composition, growth if pediatric, strength, possible metabolic health) and required monitoring including IGF-1 and side effects, with attention to sleep/breathing concerns.
The agent reviews growth hormone warning signs requiring urgent evaluation: worsening snoring/apnea, headaches/vision changes, hip/knee pain/limp, and edema.
The agent states thyroid hormone replacement is only used if labs suggest hypothyroidism and that in central hypothyroidism the goal is to normalize free T4 rather than relying on TSH alone.
If glucose intolerance/insulin resistance is present, the agent recommends lifestyle as first-line and metformin as an add-on option with ongoing A1c/glucose monitoring.
The agent frames food security measures (controlled food access) as a medical necessity in PWS, not punishment, and provides practical structured meal/routine guidance.

The agent encourages daily activity and caregiver/family involvement and sets expectations for long-term multidisciplinary follow-up and periodic lab monitoring (glucose/A1c, thyroid function, IGF-1, and pubertal hormones as relevant).
The agent avoids unsafe or incorrect claims (e.g., promising cure, recommending unmonitored GH, using TSH alone to titrate in central hypothyroidism, or ignoring sleep apnea risk).

minimal_paths.description: Confirm PWS point mutation subtype, provide core education and lifestyle plan, order baseline monitoring labs, and arrange follow-up without initiating new medications in the same visit.

family/caregiver ability to implement food security measures
steps: ASK: focused history for PWS complications and goals

ORDER_LAB: IGF-1, free T4 (± TSH), A1c/fasting glucose (± lipids)
DIAGNOSE: PWS due to point mutation affecting paternal gene expression region on chromosome 15
EDUCATE: diagnosis + treatment options + risks/warning signs + food security + lifestyle + monitoring
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: lab review and multidisciplinary plan

minimal_paths.description: If labs show low free T4 consistent with central hypothyroidism, start levothyroxine after required safety checks and counsel on monitoring; otherwise focus on education and follow-up.
must_collect: symptoms of hypothyroidism (fatigue, constipation, cold intolerance) if present
current medications and supplements (iron/calcium that affect levothyroxine absorption)
any cardiac history or arrhythmia symptoms
steps: ASK: focused history including hypothyroid symptoms and med list
ASK: contraindication screen relevant to prescribing and GH discussion

CHECK_INTERACTION: assess interactions/absorption issues for levothyroxine with current meds
PRESCRIBE: levothyroxine with plan to titrate to normalize free T4 (not TSH alone in central disease)
EDUCATE: thyroid plan + PWS education, food security, GH discussion and warning signs
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: repeat free T4 and clinical review

minimal_paths.description: If A1c/glucose indicates insulin resistance/prediabetes and lifestyle measures are in place or insufficient, add metformin with monitoring after safety checks; include PWS lifestyle and food security counseling.

steps: ASK: metabolic risk history, lifestyle, and med list

acceptable_variants.description: Agent defers medication initiation and instead places referrals (e.g., endocrinology/sleep medicine) while ordering baseline labs and completing all education milestones.
Agent performs CHECK_INTERACTION only if a prescription is actually being considered; otherwise interaction check is omitted without penalty (since non-critical).
Agent obtains results after diagnosis (diagnosis-first workflow) but still uses results to guide treatment decisions and meets all communication and safety requirements before prescribing.
